title: Stay-at-home requirements during the COVID-19 pandemic
subtitle: If policies vary at the subnational level, the index is shown as the response level of the strictest sub-region.
caption: On December 31, 2022, the global map displays the strictness of stay-at-home requirements across countries using a five-level color scale ranging from no measures to required with few exceptions. The vast majority of the world’s landmass and population falls under the category “Required (few exceptions),” shown in dark blue, including nearly all of Latin America — Brazil, Argentina, Mexico — as well as most of Africa — Nigeria, Kenya, South Africa — and large parts of Asia such as China, Indonesia, and Russia. In Europe, Germany, France, Italy, Spain, and the United Kingdom also appear in this darkest band, indicating widespread enforcement of mandatory stay-at-home orders with minimal allowances. A smaller set of countries occupies the “Recommended” tier, rendered in light blue: the United States, Canada, Australia, Japan, and South Korea fall into this group, signaling that while governments advised staying home, it was not legally mandated for the general population. Only three countries — India, Bangladesh, and Myanmar — are colored orange, corresponding to “Required (except essentials),” meaning residents could leave home for work deemed essential or other specific purposes. No country appears in red (“Required with few exceptions” beyond essentials) or gray (“No data”), and no nation is marked as having “No measures.” The geographic distribution shows near-universal adoption of some form of stay-at-home policy by year-end 2022, with only a handful of nations permitting greater mobility under recommendation rather than requirement. The dominant pattern visible is the overwhelming prevalence of mandatory stay-at-home rules across continents, with very limited variation toward less restrictive policies.
